I had pretty low expectations to simply enjoy myself and they were surpassed in how this movie fails to be coherent even if I assumed the script was in its first draft. Even if all the casting was perfect it wouldn’t save this. It’s all just dressing. The substance isn’t there. Snow is presented in contradictory ways where on one hand he’s empathetic and caring but on the other continues to advocate for the slaughter of children. I understand this is where his character has to go but I could see the writer’s hand through all of this. It makes any viewer with some critical thinking realize that they’re not experiencing something. It’s not a person developing and having an arc. It’s a puppet with the strings on full display.

Being a fan of both the books/films, I loved the accuracy depiction on most things from this film, including all the Easter eggs & the previous scores incorporated to a folk version.

Normies may not be as enthused as the book fans, given how complex and quick paced it felt throughout, ma

As a fan of this book, I was upset how very little time Clemensia was in the movie, despite being somewhat pivotal during Act 2. Many details were left out, such as how The 10th Games were wiped out of history… the funerals from the mentors and tributes prior to the Games… and Billy Toupe’s constant encounters with the Coveys. 

I only bring this up as because the GP wouldn’t understand what happened, as it was too much “tell don’t show” for all the backstories. 

There may not be another HG book or movie, although it was nice to dive back into a nostalgic yet realistic world ahead.

I love how the dualities in aesthetics between the Capitol and the Districts in this era are less overt than we have seen from the original HG series. The Capitol is an amalgam of war-torn, rebuilt Cold War/Soviet Bloc cities, with its inhabitants wearing colors that are less showy or vibrant as we seen from the original HG series, the Capitol is still reeling from the Dark Days despite winning against a much larger population. Whereas the Districts are filled with dilapidating Depression-era industrial towns, with their inhabitants wearing well-crafted, yet dirty clothing.  And the Hunger Games just in its 10th year, it appears less as a celebrated event, but rather recompense for the Districts’ defiance, one that is still exercised through District 12’s selection of Lucy Gray Baird, a symbolic role reversal of hunter-turned-performer Katniss Everdeen. It is not enough for the Capitol to fully implement its mission power and oppression without the help of its future ruler, Coriolanus Snow.

I loved the books, and whilst I loved the earlier films, I felt the later films lost something along the way. And when the films came out what happened? The fanbase fixated on the romance and were desensitised to the horror of what they were seeing. The lack of awareness was... off putting.

However, I feel like this might just be a return to form. I'm intrigued.

SPOILERS 




One of the little things I loved about the book was how this Panem feels like a bridge between our world and trilogy Panem which is so distant it was easy to forget that it is future North America. In The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes, there are little tidbits of real-life culture such as Oh My Darling, Clementine, and the remembrance of Woodsworth and other poets. They are not overwhelming or distracting, and so when they come up they feel comforting/nostalgic. They feel like bits of hope in a world gone to hell. The contrast between their comforting nature (to the reader) and the hate that Snow develops for them is great dramatic irony. These real-life songs and poems really help the Covey feel like they are from another time period. I think it also highlights another evil side of Snow. His hatred of music feels more personal when it is directed to songs we recognize. The suppression of music starts at the end of the book. It is something that Snow welcomes and there is the strong assumption that he continues that when he comes to power. It is tragic that he seems to have crushed the remembrance of our music and poems by the trilogy, but inspiring to know the spirit of the Covey lives on and eventually takes him down. I hope this comes across well in the movie. I'm optimistic given how important music is to the story and how it seems they will at least have to include Wordsworth's Lucy Gray poem

If you are reading this, a new revolution should be on its way. A complete disbelief into one's priorities and way of living. 
And reconstruction into something that may be the answer we all have been looking for. 
They trapped us. Smartphones, social media, content creators, sponsors, programming, A.I's.
History has proven to us that when we fight. 
When we fight each other, family, friends, neighbors customers, bosses, states, countries, we only lose. 
They profit from us bleeding into each others hands. 
This is not just a movie... whether you like it or not it's a message, it's a story. Inspired by us, dear humans.
Cause that's why art exists. 
And now more than ever, we, should not point fingers at each other but we should all point them to the people who say they are the face of the countries themselves, who say they protect us and they look out for us. They lie. We all know better. We all understand better. We should not be afraid of their money, or their crosses. 
Because they are not even real, they don't understand the power they have in their hands. Do you even believe in life anymore? 
If you feel alone, hold on to nature, because we came from it. We are all united, and we do only have one enemy, and it's not a fictional character named Snow. 
What are you gonna teach your kids? how to love? how to be respectful? how to not be anxious or depressed or scared? 
Let's fight for our right to live a peaceful life. 
Or do you still believe that this rock floating in space gives a damn about capitalism or communism ? 
There are solutions. It's not utopic. 
The ods may still be in our favor.

I read the book twice, I just hope its not all a bunch of woke crap. There was never a mention of Casco Highbottom being a dwarf, with all due respect to the awesome Mr Dinklage. Its just that when you have a great book, the pointless additions, subtractions and changes that the creatively starved and imaginatively bankrupt people at the studio think will "improve" an already great story, or worse yet, lets reimagine it to be closer to a world that represents our own. 
For cripes sake, its already a dystopian world, where people judge each other on their family name, their status, their connections, their accent, their haircuts, their role, their job, where they came from, the cut of their clothes, the food they eat, every action is judged and anyone stepping out of line is severely punished. Taking food from the dining hall is an act that will get you expelled, your house taken away and you will lose everything. 
Does this sound like a world that gives a damn about "equity" "equality", opportunity and representation?!?!?!?!  
Its the farthest you could ever get from it! 
If you read the book, you can see that they are about the most prejudiced, racist, misogynist, abusive, pedophiliac, unforgiving people you could ever meet. (talking about the Capitol people, for the most part. Although the districts have their hate and rage for strangers, as well) 
So you could imagine them feeding a dwarf to the dogs just for the fun of it, or sending anyone that does not conform to the Capitols standards to the districts, to a work gang, cutting their tongue out for almost no offence other that saying the wrong thing to the wrong person. 
All that being said, "and true to the book and the story" the younger kids in this book do have many apprehensions to the killing and often wonder why it is even  happening, since the war is over. At this point, for the first few years of the Hunger Games, its not even very popular with people, up to this point there has been very little televised stuff, no gamboling, no betting, no endorsements, very little coverage in the districts. So its not really popular up to this point. 
In any case, this is not a movie that needed to be "woke" in any stretch of the imagination. 
But, a lot will hinge on Lucy Gray and Snow. Its really about the dynamic between then that carry the best scenes.

"The Hunger Games: The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes" is a novel by American author Suzanne Collins. It was published in 2020 as a prequel to Collins' popular "Hunger Games" trilogy, which consists of "The Hunger Games," "Catching Fire," and "Mockingjay."

Set 64 years before the events of the original trilogy, "The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes" follows the story of a young Coriolanus Snow, who later becomes the tyrannical President of Panem. The book explores the early years of Snow, before he became the villain of the original trilogy. The novel takes place during the 10th annual Hunger Games and follows Snow's involvement as a mentor to the tribute from District 12, a young girl named Lucy Gray Baird.

"The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes" received mixed reviews from critics and fans, with some praising the exploration of Snow's character and others feeling that the book did not live up to the original trilogy. Nevertheless, the book became a bestseller and was well-received by many fans of "The Hunger Games" franchise.

The novel explores themes of power, ambition, morality, and the corrupting influence of authority. It provides insights into the origins of the Hunger Games and the Capitol's control over the districts. While the original trilogy focused on Katniss Everdeen's perspective, "The Ballad of Songbirds & Snakes" offers a different viewpoint by examining the life and experiences of a young President Snow.

"The Hunger Games: The Ballad of Songbirds & Snakes" was published in May 2020 and has been well-received by fans of the original series. However, as of my knowledge cutoff in September 2021, there have been no official announcements regarding a film adaptation of the prequel novel.

I know I'm going to be a minority in this view point.  But between Dinklage and the constant "Snow" reference in the trailer, this feels more like Game of Thrones than The Hunger Games. And maybe that is the point.  But the mannerisms shown in the trailer also point to another franchise.  Harry Potter.  Seriously, the way people talk.  The overly dark backgrounds, I half expect Voldemort to make an appearance and people start to waving wands around.

Again, maybe this was the point.  But why would you have to make inferred references to other franchises when The Hunger Games did so well in the box office?  I suspect this movie will do well because it has The Hunger Games brand behind it, but I just am not feeling this movie at all.

I literally wonder if the creative team behind this movie know anything about pop culture or even how to write a script and sought inspiration in this created since 2000.  In short, their entire creative team is under the age of 25.

A woman is an adult female human. Prior to adulthood, a female human is referred to as a girl (a female child or adolescent). Denoting the sex that can bear offspring or produce eggs, distinguished biologically by the production of gametes (ova) which can be fertilized by male gametes. Women inherit a pair of X chromosomes from their parents, and are capable of pregnancy and giving birth from puberty until menopause. More generally, sex differentiation of the female fetus is governed by the lack of a present, or functioning, SRY-gene on either one of the respective sex chromosomes. Female anatomy is distinguished from male anatomy by the female reproductive system, which includes the ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, vagina, and vulva. The adult female pelvis is wider, the hips broader, and the breasts larger than that of adult males. Women have significantly less facial and other body hair, have a higher body fat composition, and are on average shorter and less muscular than men.

A man is an adult male human.
Prior to adulthood, a male human is referred to as a boy (a male child or adolescent). 
Like most other male mammals, a man's genome inherits an X chromosome from the mother and a Y chromosome from the father. Sex differentiation of the male fetus is governed by the SRY gene on the Y chromosome. During puberty, hormones which stimulate androgen production result in the development of secondary sexual characteristics, thus exhibiting greater differences between the sexes. These include greater muscle mass, the growth of facial hair and a lower body fat composition.,,,.....

So: 
Saw the first half of the trailer with the audio cut. Bought the book a day later. Finished the book in two days. Here again to watch the full thing. In the least-spoiled way I can, I’ll point out the things that interested me.
1. Lucy Gray has the accent I read for her. Perfect.
2. The snakes fail to resemble a certain something, but they look unnerving anyway. Good. 
3. Doctor Gaul has a blue eye. Wonder why they decided on that for her, but she’s one… _eccentric_ piece of work. I’m sure there’s an aesthetical reason if not one to be explained.
4. If the man who plays Lucky isn’t Stanley Tucci, then they did a really good job finding someone who looked similar. 
5. Intriguing to see Peter Dinklage among the cast, since I’ve always seen him in roles that carry an intimidating presence with them, though this character he’s playing is more sad than anything. 
6. The man playing Snow _looks_ like he could kill with a thought. I immediately don’t trust him. Flawless execution. 

There are many more things I want to say, but they’d be spoilers, and there are so many comments above and below mine that are probably doing just that. So if mine happens to be the first one you see, keep that in mind.

This book was one of the best books I have read in a series. It really does an excellent job at explaining so much about the antagonist President Snow and connects the things he hates and why he hates them (The Mocking Jay, District 12, "underdogs", marrying a woman he would never love, not being "wasteful" in his own way, the significance of white roses, etc.). It brings a fullness and an explanation for his "cruelty with boundaries" to his character and by the end of the book you understand the deep complexity of his character and how his past impacted and propelled his rise to power. This book literally ties EVERYTHING together for the reader from all three previous novels to get a fuller understanding of the creation and development of the Hunger Games to how the Capital came to be like it is under President Snow.

 I highly recommend you read the novel and I REALLY REALLY hope they don't mess up the intricate details that are vital to understanding his character in this movie adaptation!!

ok hear me! I am from Poland and let's face it, the history of our country was and still is to some extent totalitarian. As a teenager, I read the Hunger Games books several times. When the movie series was about to come out, I was thrilled. The movies were really good. It is true that they did not show the whole charakter of Katniss, but they did a great job of showing the politics, war and toxicity of the post-apocalyptic world.

As a 22-year-old, when I heard that a new book was being created, I was overjoyed. I was afraid, however, that it would be written like the previous ones - for people in their teenage years. While reading the book I realized that it is a nod to older fans and is aimed at an older audience. In my opinion, it is MUCH BETTER THAN THE ENTIRE TRILOGY. We've been reading it aloud with our partner for a few months before going to bed and it still evokes great emotions in us.

I hope the movie will appeal to an older audience, the kind that grew up on these books.

ANYWAY, I AM JUST AS EXCITED AS I WAS WHEN I WAS 13 YEARS OLD. AAAAAAA I CAN'T WAIT

What's bothering me is the color and aesthetics of the film. The colors look too sharp and too clean. I wanted a past that looked more worn out.

If you were to compare the first Hunger Games movie to this one, Hunger Games looks like those movies in the 80s, which is what this film should've look like.

I'd figure if we were to get a glimpse of a much younger Snow, the color and conditions of that time would look more dark and bleak. Like a time from when there used to be gladiators. Just barren land, that's desolate. Where the people look very poor and malnourished. And those that were of higher society and worked in the government would look more like eccentric scientists with flashy vibrant colors. Not too futuristic yet.

We saw the harsh conditions all the districts were living in in the first Hunger Games movie. I'd figure during Snow's teen years it would be even worse conditions than our first intro to Hunger Games.

Anyone else feel the same way? I'm sure the story is gonna be great but the while look and feel of it is throwing me off a bit. I'll still watch it though just for the backstory.
